entry.title: Creating a BW workspace using a composite provider
entry.content:
I want to create a BW workspace using a composite provider that joins two ADSOs, but I encountered an issue.  I am working in BW4 and want to use a composite provider as my main provider in the workspace settings of RSWSP. The composite provider is a join of two ADSOs.  I have tried multiple ways to get around this.  First was to create a Union node in the composite directly above a Join and assign it to the main provider of RSWSP.  Only then can you see it in the Central Providers section.  Second was to do a Joule suggested, and that was to drag in the Composite provider in the Central Providers section along with the two ADSOs, but the composite provider is not available unless it is in the Settings for main provider section first.  Even there, you cannot add additional ADSOs once the Settings for main provider has been established.  Please see attached.

 

I should not have to create a new model.  The Composite model already exists.  I am just trying to use what is already built, not reconstruct what is already built.  We need this tool so we can allow our Power Users to use the Workspace Designer Query functionality.  We would assign this capability as a self-service capability for power users, that could help generate queries for our infoconsumber base.  Giving them access to Eclipse is not the ideal state. 

 

Steps;

Original Scenario cannot be used as a main provider on the Setting Tab of RSWSP

Workaround #1, tried to put a Union as the top of the new Scenario.

Workspace designer does allow it in the Settings for main provider:

However, when looking at this in central providers, it remains in Error:

Attempting work around #2, tried to add ADSOs after establishing Settings for main provider.

Please also note that there is no “Composite” provider to select from in the Central Providers area.

entry.title: From Whiteboard to SAP BTP: Building an Open Source AI Architecture Diagram Generator
entry.content:
Every SAP BTP architect knows the ritual. You finish a whiteboard session, photograph the sketch on your phone, and then spend the next hour manually recreating it in draw.io — hunting through the SAP icon library, wiring up the right service boxes, getting the boundary zones right. The diagram ends up taking longer than the actual architecture decision.

We built a tool to fix that. Sketch to SAP BTP takes a whiteboard photo or a Mermaid diagram and converts it into an official SAP BTP Solution Diagram in .drawio format — complete with the right SAP service icons, boundary zones, and connector styles. It runs in seconds, on your own BTP subaccount, using your own Anthropic API key.

Today we're releasing it as open source under Apache 2.0.

The tool UI — file upload tab and Mermaid input tab side by side

What it does

The tool accepts two kinds of input:

  • Whiteboard sketch — a photo (PNG, JPEG, WebP, GIF up to 10 MB) of a hand-drawn architecture
  • Mermaid diagram — any flowchart or graph definition

From either input, it runs a four-step pipeline:

Identify components → Map to SAP catalog → Review architecture → Generate .drawio

The output is a ready-to-open .drawio file with proper SAP service icons, swimlane boundary zones, and styled connectors. Drop it into diagrams.net or the draw.io desktop app and it opens correctly without any manual adjustment.

The technical implementation

Step 1 — Claude Vision extracts the architecture

For sketch input, we use Claude's vision capability with structured tool use. Rather than asking Claude to describe the image in natural language and then parsing that output, we define a tool schema that forces the model to return a structured JSON representation of the architecture directly.

// srv/agents/vision.js — simplified
const tools = [{
  name: "extract_architecture",
  input_schema: {
    type: "object",
    properties: {
      title: { type: "string" },
      components: {
        type: "array",
        items: {
          type: "object",
          properties: {
            id: { type: "string" },
            label: { type: "string" },
            type: { type: "string" }
          }
        }
      },
      connections: { /* ... */ }
    }
  }
}];

This approach eliminates prompt-parsing fragility. The model either calls the tool with valid structured data or it doesn't — there's no regex or text parsing required on our side. Claude Sonnet 4.6 with vision handles even rough whiteboard photos reliably.

A whiteboard sketch being uploaded

Step 2 — Mermaid gets a dedicated parser (no LLM needed)

When the input is a Mermaid diagram, we skip the vision model entirely. Mermaid is structured text — parsing it deterministically is faster, cheaper, and more accurate than asking an LLM to interpret it. Our parser handles:

  • All flowchart directions: LR, TD, RL, BT
  • Edge types: -->, -->|label|, -.->, ==>
  • Chained edges: A --> B --> C
  • Cylinder shape [(label)] for databases
  • YAML front-matter and %% title: for diagram titles

Mermaid parsing costs exactly $0 and returns results in milliseconds.

Step 3 — Deterministic SAP service catalog mapping

Once we have a list of labelled components, we map each one to an official SAP BTP service using sap_service_catalog.json — a bundled catalog of SAP services with their draw.io style metadata. Matching runs in three tiers:

Mapping confidence tiers Exact match — the label directly matches a service name or alias (e.g. "SAP HANA Cloud" → hana-cloud)
Alias match — a known abbreviation or alternative name matches (e.g. "HANA" → hana-cloud)
Type fallback — no match found, but the component type (database, queue, API) suggests a generic placeholder

This is all deterministic code — no LLM involved in the mapping step. It's fast, predictable, and easy to extend by adding entries to sap_service_catalog.json.

Step 4 — Architecture review with Claude Haiku

Before generating the final diagram, we pass the mapped architecture to Claude Haiku 4.5 for a lightweight best-practices review. It checks for common issues — missing integration layers, direct database access without a service tier, absent authentication components — and returns structured warnings.

We use Haiku for this step deliberately: the review prompt is short and the expected output is structured JSON warnings. Using a smaller model here keeps the cost per run under a cent while still catching real architectural issues.

The validation results are available two ways: embedded in the downloaded .drawio response as HTTP headers (X-Validation-Summary, X-Warning-Count, X-Missing-Services), or as a standalone JSON response via POST /validate-only if you want the review without generating a diagram.

The validation panel showing detected SAP components with confidence chips and architecture review warnings

Step 5 — draw.io XML generation

The final step converts the mapped, validated architecture into draw.io XML. SAP publishes an official icon set for BTP services — we bundle these as base64 SVG in sap_icon_index.json and embed them directly into the draw.io cell styles. The generator places components in boundary zones (Integration Suite, Extension Suite, etc.) based on service category, positions them using a topology-aware BFS layout — components are assigned columns based on their graph depth from actor/entry-point nodes, preserving the left-to-right data flow of the original sketch — and wires up connectors.

The output is standard draw.io XML — it opens in diagrams.net, the draw.io desktop app, or any tool that reads the format.

The generated .drawio file open in diagrams.net showing SAP service icons, boundary zones, and connectors

API key resolution — three sources, one priority order

The app resolves the Anthropic API key from these sources in priority order:

Source When used
VCAP_SERVICES.aicoreSAP AI Core / GenAI Hub binding
VCAP_SERVICES.credential-storeBTP Credential Store — recommended for production
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env varLocal dev and quick CF deployments

This means the tool works with SAP's own AI Core infrastructure for organisations already using GenAI Hub, while remaining simple to run locally with a direct API key.

Cost per run

Step Model Approx. cost
Sketch analysisclaude-sonnet-4-6~$0.013
Architecture reviewclaude-haiku-4-5~$0.003
Mermaid parsingNo LLM$0.00

All costs are charged to your own Anthropic account — Aditheos has zero visibility into your usage or spend.

Why we open sourced it

The honest answer: a commercial SaaS model doesn't fit this tool well.

Enterprise SAP architects handle sensitive network topology and system landscape data. They will deploy an open source tool to their own private BTP space without hesitation. Getting the same data approved to flow through an external SaaS is a legal review and a procurement process. The paywall would block the exact people the tool is most useful to.

There's also the catalog maintenance problem. SAP's BTP service catalog changes constantly — new services, renamed services, deprecated plans. If this were a closed product, we'd be solely responsible for keeping sap_service_catalog.json up to date. Open source means the community can submit pull requests when a service launches or changes. That's a better outcome for everyone.

The most impactful contribution Adding new entries to data/sap_service_catalog.json — service names, aliases, draw.io style metadata — directly improves mapping accuracy for every user. If your organisation uses a service that isn't mapping correctly, a PR to the catalog is the highest-leverage fix.

Getting started in under five minutes

Local development:

git clone https://github.com/aditheos/sketch-to-sap-btp-cap.git
cd sketch-to-sap-btp-cap

cp .env.example .env
# Add your ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to .env

npm install
npm run dev   # → http://localhost:4004

Deploy to your own BTP subaccount:

npm install
npm run build

cf login -a <your-api-endpoint>
cf target -o <your-org> -s <your-space>
cf deploy sketch-to-sap-btp_*.mtar

cf set-env sketch-to-sap-btp-srv ANTHROPIC_API_KEY sk-ant-...
cf restage sketch-to-sap-btp-srv

No additional BTP services required. The deployment is a single Cloud Foundry app. No HANA, no approuter, no IAS — just the CAP backend serving the UI and handling conversions.

What's next

A few directions the community could take this:

  • SAP BAS extension — a plugin that runs the conversion inside Business Application Studio, where architects actually spend their time
  • Two-way generation — from diagram to CAP/BTP project scaffold (the reverse direction)
  • More output formats — PowerPoint-ready slides, Confluence diagrams, PlantUML
  • Expanded catalog — deeper coverage of SAP Integration Suite, SAP Build, and industry-specific services

The repository is at github.com/aditheos/sketch-to-sap-btp-cap. Issues and pull requests are open
Source Reference: Sketch to SAP BTP Diagram

entry.title: Connecting Academia and SAP Through the SAP Campus Ambassador Community
entry.content:
At Academic Partnerships, we believe that meaningful collaboration between academia and industry can create powerful learning opportunities for students. Professors are constantly looking for ways to bring real-world perspectives into the classroom, connect theory with practice and help students better understand how technology and business come together in today’s organizations.

One way SAP supports this connection is through the SAP Campus Ambassador Community.

 

What is the SAP Campus Ambassador Community?

The SAP Campus Ambassador Community is an internal community of SAP colleagues who are interested in supporting education, academic engagement and early talent development.

These colleagues come from different teams, regions, roles and areas of expertise across SAP. They are not part of one single department and they do not work full-time for Academic Partnerships. Instead, they volunteer their time and knowledge because they want to contribute to student learning and strengthen the connection between SAP and educational institutions.

Today, the community brings together more than 2,400 SAP colleagues worldwide, creating a diverse network of professionals who can help bring SAP perspectives closer to students and faculty.

 

How can Campus Ambassadors support faculty?

SAP Campus Ambassadors can support professors and educational institutions in different ways, depending on the academic need, topic, timing and volunteer availability.

For example, ambassadors may help by:

  • bringing SAP perspectives into the classroom through guest lectures or short sessions;
  • sharing professional experience on topics such as analytics, ERP, cloud, artificial intelligence, sustainability, innovation or career development;
  • supporting student engagement moments, such as workshops, student events, project presentations or career conversations;
  • helping faculty explore how SAP University Alliances resources can be connected to existing courses or programs;
  • serving as a bridge to SAP knowledge, people, initiatives and opportunities.

The goal is not to create a one-size-fits-all model. Each collaboration can look different depending on the professor’s context, the students’ needs and the availability of SAP colleagues.

 

A volunteer community with real impact

An important point to keep in mind is that SAP Campus Ambassadors are volunteers. They are SAP employees with their own roles and responsibilities and their participation depends on their interest, expertise, and availability.

This means that the community is not a dedicated delivery team or a guaranteed service. However, when there is a clear academic need and a realistic collaboration idea, Campus Ambassadors can become a valuable bridge between SAP and academia.

Through this community, faculty can create meaningful moments for students, moments where they hear from professionals, explore real business and technology topics and better understand how SAP solutions and careers connect to what they are learning.

 

How to explore a collaboration

If you are a professor or faculty member interested in connecting with the SAP Campus Ambassador Community, the best starting point is to think about your academic need.

For example:

  • What course, program or student group would benefit from an SAP perspective?
  • What would you like students to learn, experience or explore?
  • What type of format would make sense: a guest lecture, a short workshop, a Business Builders session, a career conversation or a connection with an SAP expert?
  • When would this ideally happen and how much time would be needed?

A clear and focused request makes it easier for our Academic Partnerships team to understand the opportunity and explore whether there may be a good fit with a Campus Ambassador or another SAP colleague.

To explore a possible collaboration, please contact the Academic Partnerships team at universityalliances@sap.com with your request. Our team will review the opportunity and explore the best possible connection based on the topic, timing, scope and volunteer availability.

 

Have you already worked with an SAP Campus Ambassador in your classes? Let us know here in the comments!

entry.title: Building and Deploying Your First MCP Server on SAP BTP
entry.content:
1. Understanding MCP

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a standard that lets an AI model call external tools and read data in a structured way. Think of it like a USB standard for AI — just as USB lets any device plug into any computer, MCP lets any tool plug into any AI model.

The Three Primitives

An MCP server can expose three types of capabilities:

Primitive

Purpose

Analogy

Tools

Functions the AI can call and execute — like pressing a button

A menu item in a restaurant

Resources

Data the AI can read as context — like a reference document

A reference manual

Prompts

Reusable prompt templates the AI or user can invoke

A saved workflow

In this tutorial we focus on Tools — the most powerful primitive for SAP integration.

Architecture

The flow is simple:

 

 

 

 

 

The user types a natural language request. The AI reads the tool descriptions (docstrings), decides which tool to call, the MCP server executes the OData call, and returns the formatted result to the AI — which then replies to the user.

2. Prerequisites

Before we start, ensure you have the following installed and configured:

Requirement

Version

Purpose

Python

3.10+

Runtime for the MCP server

VS Code

Latest

IDE for development and debugging

Node.js

18+

Required for the MCP Inspector browser UI

CF CLI

v8+

Deploy to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry

SAP BTP Account

—

Cloud Foundry environment with a space

Install the VS Code Python extension from the Extensions marketplace (Ctrl+Shift+X → search Python → Install).

3. Setting Up VS Code

Step 1 — Create the Project Folder

Open VS Code and go to File → Open Folder. Create a new folder called my_first_mcp and open it. Your VS Code Explorer should show MY_FIRST_MCP at the top.

Step 2 — Open the Terminal

Go to Terminal → New Terminal. A terminal panel opens at the bottom of VS Code.

Step 3 — Create a Virtual Environment

A virtual environment isolates your project's Python packages from the rest of your system — each project has its own clean set of dependencies.

python3 -m venv .venv --upgrade-deps

Activate it:

Mac / Linux:

source .venv/bin/activate

Windows:

.venv\Scripts\activate

Your terminal prompt changes to show (.venv) — this confirms the environment is active.

VS Code may prompt 'Do you want to select this environment as the workspace interpreter?' — click Yes. This ensures the debugger uses the correct Python.

Step 4 — Install Dependencies

pip install "mcp[cli]" httpx

This installs two packages:

  • mcp[cli] — FastMCP server and the mcp dev Inspector command
  • httpx — an async HTTP client for making OData calls

Verify the installation:

mcp version

You should see the mcp version number printed.

4. Building the MCP Server

Create a new file in your project folder called server_trippin.py. This will be our complete MCP server.

Step 1 — The Server Skeleton

Every MCP server starts with the same three lines:

import httpx

from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP

mcp = FastMCP("TripPin Server")

BASE_URL = "https://services.odata.org/TripPinRESTierService"

FastMCP is the high-level framework that handles the MCP protocol for us. We give the server a name and set the base URL for our OData service.

Step 2 — Understanding the Tool Pattern

Every tool follows the same pattern:

@mcp.tool()

async def tool_name(parameter: type) -> str:

    """Docstring — the AI reads this to know when to use this tool."""

    # make HTTP call

    # parse response

    # return formatted string

 

Part

What it does

@mcp.tool()

Registers this function as an MCP tool

Function name

Becomes the tool name the AI calls

Parameters + types

Become the tool's input schema automatically

Docstring

The AI reads this to decide when to use the tool

Return value

Sent back to the AI as the tool result

Step 3 — The GET Tools (Read Operations)

Let's add our first two tools — listing and fetching people:

@mcp.tool()

async def get_people(top: int = 5) -> str:

    """Get a list of people. top = how many to return (default 5)."""

    url = f"{BASE_URL}/People"

    params = {"$format": "json", "$top": top,

              "$select": "UserName,FirstName,LastName,Gender"}

    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:

        response = await client.get(url, params=params)

    people = response.json()["value"]

    lines = []

    for p in people:

        lines.append(f"Username:{p['UserName']:<20} {p['FirstName']} {p['LastName']}")

    return "\n".join(lines)



@mcp.tool()

async def get_person(username: str) -> str:

    """Get details of a single person by username."""

    url = f"{BASE_URL}/People('{username}')"

    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:

        response = await client.get(url, params={"$format": "json"})

    if response.status_code == 404:

        return f"Person '{username}' not found."

    p = response.json()

    return (f"Username  : {p['UserName']}\n"

            f"First Name: {p['FirstName']}\n"

            f"Last Name : {p['LastName']}\n"

            f"Emails    : {', '.join(p.get('Emails', []))}")

Notice we use async/await with httpx.AsyncClient(). This is important — MCP tools should be async so the server can handle multiple requests efficiently.

Step 4 — The POST Tool (Create)

@mcp.tool()

async def create_person(username: str, first_name: str, last_name: str) -> str:

    """Create a new person in TripPin."""

    url = f"{BASE_URL}/People"

    payload = {

        "UserName": username,

        "FirstName": first_name,

        "LastName": last_name,

        "Emails": [], "AddressInfo": [], "Features": []

    }

    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:

        response = await client.post(

            url, json=payload,

            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"}

        )

    if response.status_code in (200, 201):

        p = response.json()

        return f"Created: {p['UserName']} — {p['FirstName']} {p['LastName']}"

    return f"Failed. HTTP {response.status_code}: {response.text[:200]}"

Step 5 — The PATCH Tool (Update)

@mcp.tool()

async def update_person(username: str, first_name: str = "", last_name: str = "") -> str:

    """Update an existing person's first or last name."""

    url = f"{BASE_URL}/People('{username}')"

    payload = {}

    if first_name: payload["FirstName"] = first_name

    if last_name:  payload["LastName"]  = last_name

    if not payload: return "Nothing to update."

    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:

        response = await client.patch(

            url, json=payload,

            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"}

        )

    if response.status_code in (200, 204):

        return f"Person '{username}' updated successfully."

    return f"Failed. HTTP {response.status_code}"

Step 6 — The DELETE Tool

@mcp.tool()

async def delete_person(username: str) -> str:

    """Delete a person by username."""

    url = f"{BASE_URL}/People('{username}')"

    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:

        response = await client.delete(url)

    if response.status_code in (200, 204):

        return f"Person '{username}' deleted successfully."

    return f"Failed. HTTP {response.status_code}"

Step 7 — The Entry Point

Add this at the very bottom of server_trippin.py:

if __name__ == "__main__":

    import os, asyncio

    transport = os.getenv("MCP_TRANSPORT", "stdio")

    port = int(os.getenv("PORT", 8080))

    if transport == "sse":

        mcp.settings.port = port

        mcp.settings.host = "0.0.0.0"

        asyncio.run(mcp.run_sse_async())

    else:

        asyncio.run(mcp.run_stdio_async())

This block handles both local development (stdio) and BTP deployment (SSE) using an environment variable. When running locally with mcp dev, it uses stdio. When deployed to BTP with MCP_TRANSPORT=sse, it switches to SSE mode.

5. Testing Locally with MCP Inspector

MCP Inspector is a browser-based UI that lets you call every tool interactively — without needing Claude Desktop or any AI client. It is the fastest way to test and debug your tools.

Step 1 — Run the Server

In the VS Code terminal (with .venv active):

mcp dev server_trippin.py

You will see output like:

Starting MCP inspector...

Proxy server listening on localhost:6277

MCP Inspector is up and running at:

http://localhost:6274/?MCP_PROXY_AUTH_TOKEN=...

Open the URL shown in your browser.

Step 2 — Connect and Test

In the MCP Inspector browser UI:

  • Click Connect to connect to your running server
  • Click the Tools tab — you should see all 5 tools listed
  • Click get_people → enter top=5 → click Run

 enter top=5 → click Run

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

You should see a formatted list of people returned from the TripPin OData service.

If the Inspector cannot connect, check that the terminal shows the server running and that you copied the full URL including the token parameter.

Step 3 — Test All CRUD Operations

Tool

Test Input

Expected Result

get_people

top=5

List of 5 people with username and name

get_person

username=angelhuffman

Full details of Angel Huffman

create_person

username=testuser, first_name=Test, last_name=User

Confirmation with new user details

update_person

username=testuser, first_name=Updated

Update successful message

delete_person

username=testuser

Deletion successful message

The TripPin service uses session-based URLs. If you get a redirect error, open the TripPin URL in your browser, copy the session ID from the redirected URL (e.g. /TripPinRESTierService/(S(xxxxxxxxxx))/), and update BASE_URL in your server.

6. Deploying to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry

Now that our MCP server works locally, let's deploy it to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry so it can be accessed remotely by any MCP-compatible AI client.

Step 1 — Create requirements.txt

Create a file called requirements.txt in your project folder:

mcp[cli]==1.2.0

httpx

uvicorn

We pin mcp to version 1.2.0 specifically. Later versions (1.5+) include strict host header validation that conflicts with SAP BTP's GoRouter reverse proxy, causing 421 Misdirected Request errors. Version 1.2.0 is stable and works correctly on BTP.

Step 2 — Create manifest.yml

Create a file called manifest.yml:

applications:

  - name: mcp-trippin-server

    buildpacks:

      - python_buildpack

    memory: 256M

    instances: 1

    command: python server_trippin.py

    env:

      MCP_TRANSPORT: sse

      DANGEROUSLY_OMIT_AUTH: "true"

Key settings explained:

  • python_buildpack — tells BTP to use the Python runtime
  • memory: 256M — sufficient for a lightweight MCP server
  • MCP_TRANSPORT: sse — switches our server to SSE mode on BTP
  • DANGEROUSLY_OMIT_AUTH — required for the Inspector to connect without token auth

Step 3 — Log in to Cloud Foundry

cf login -a https://api.cf.<your-region>.hana.ondemand.com

Enter your BTP email and password when prompted, then select your org and space.

Step 4 — Deploy

cf push mcp-trippin-server -f manifest.yml

BTP will:

  • Upload your project files
  • Download the Python buildpack
  • Install all packages from requirements.txt
  • Start the server on port 8080

This takes approximately 2-3 minutes. When complete you will see:

state     since          cpu    memory

#0   running   2026-07-18   0.2%   45M of 256M

Step 5 — Verify Deployment

cf apps

You should see your app with status started and 1/1 instances running:

name                  requested state   instances   memory   routes

mcp-trippin-server    started           web:1/1     256M     mcp-trippin-server.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

7. Testing the Deployed Server

With the server live on BTP, let's connect the MCP Inspector to the remote URL.

Step 1 — Run MCP Inspector

npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector

Step 2 — Connect to BTP

In the Inspector browser UI:

  • Change the transport dropdown from stdio to SSE
  • Enter your BTP server URL:
https://mcp-trippin-server.cfapps.<your-region>.hana.ondemand.com/sse

Click Connect

You should see the Tools tab appear with all 5 tools listed.

Step 3 — Test a Live Tool Call

Click get_people, enter top=5, and click Run. You should see live data returned from the TripPin OData service through your BTP-hosted MCP server.

You can also monitor your live server logs from the terminal: cf logs mcp-trippin-server — this streams all incoming requests and responses in real time.

Checking the BTP Cockpit

You can also verify your deployment in the SAP BTP Cockpit:

  • Log in to cockpit.btp.cloud.sap
  • Navigate to your subaccount → Cloud Foundry → Spaces → your space
  • Click Applications — you will see mcp-trippin-server with status Running
  • Click the app to see memory usage, logs, and environment variables

8. Key Lessons Learned

Deploying MCP servers to SAP BTP has some specific considerations worth highlighting:

Lesson

Detail

Pin MCP to 1.2.0

MCP 1.5+ has strict host validation incompatible with BTP's GoRouter. Pin to 1.2.0 in requirements.txt.

Use SSE not Streamable HTTP

BTP's GoRouter blocks Streamable HTTP (/mcp endpoint) with 421 errors. SSE (/sse endpoint) works correctly.

Port must be 8080

BTP Cloud Foundry always sets the PORT environment variable to 8080. Read it with int(os.getenv('PORT', 8080)).

Use mcp.settings for config

Set mcp.settings.port and mcp.settings.host before calling run_sse_async() — do not pass them as arguments.

Debug with cf logs

Use cf logs <app-name> --recent to see the last 100 lines, or cf logs <app-name> for live streaming.

Session URLs in TripPin

TripPin redirects to session URLs. If you get redirect errors, update BASE_URL with the session ID from the redirected URL.

 

Conclusion

The Model Context Protocol represents a fundamental shift in how we think about enterprise AI integration. Instead of building complex AI workflows that understand your SAP data model, you simply wrap your existing OData services in a few lines of Python — and any AI model can now interact with your SAP system in natural language.

What we built today is just the beginning. A maintenance engineer can now ask "show me all overdue work orders for plant PL01" instead of navigating through Fiori screens. A planner can say "release order 1000001" instead of executing a transaction. The AI handles the intent — your MCP server handles the execution.

The pattern you learned here scales to any SAP service. The same @mcp.tool() decorator, the same httpx async client, the same OData response parsing — whether you are calling TripPin, Northwind, or your production iSAM Maintenance Foundation service on S/4HANA.

SAP BTP Cloud Foundry gives you a reliable, enterprise-grade hosting platform for your MCP servers — keeping your tools close to your SAP landscape, inside your security boundary, and manageable through standard CF tooling you already know.

We are at an early but exciting moment. MCP is rapidly becoming the standard way AI agents interact with enterprise systems. As SAP developers, we are uniquely positioned to build these bridges — combining decades of SAP domain knowledge with modern AI tooling.

I hope this tutorial gives you the foundation to start building. Try replacing TripPin with one of your own SAP OData services, deploy it to BTP, and connect it to Claude Desktop. The moment your AI calls a real SAP transaction in natural language for the first time is genuinely exciting.

If you have questions, run into issues, or build something interesting — drop a comment below. I would love to see what the SAP community creates with MCP.

Happy building.

#MCP  #ModelContextProtocol  #SAPBTP  #CloudFoundry  #Python  #GenerativeAI  #AIAgents  #SAPDevelopers  #LLM  #OData 

@MohitBansal @AndreasForster @MichaelStill @merbinjanselm @yoramfriedman @Mohan-Sharma

entry.title: CDS is taking more time to load in Production
entry.content:
Hi ,

I have a CDS which is taking more than 40 seconds and sometime even more time to load the data or filter the data.

  1. Is there any way in ABAP CDS to achieve behavior similar to SAP table buffering for small, rarely changing configuration/customizing tables, so that repeated accesses are served from a buffer/cache instead of triggering database reads each time?
  2. If the data has already been fetched from the CDS view and we apply an additional filter for records that are already present in the fetched result set, the CDS query appears to trigger another database call. Is there a way to avoid this repeated DB access and reuse the already fetched data, for example by filtering it in memory or through some caching mechanism?

    Regards,

    Abhi

entry.title: "Monitor Production Orders" — Why Your Default View Doesn't Load on Launch (It's Not a Bug)
entry.content:
If you've set up a personalized view in the Monitor Production Orders MRP View app (App ID F0266A), marked it as Default with Apply Automatically enabled, and still see the Standard view load every time you open the app — you're not imagining it, and it's not misconfigured. It's expected SAP Fiori elements behavior, and there's a straightforward explanation.

The Symptom

  1. Go to Manage Views in the app.
  2. Create a new view (e.g., "MyLayout"), set it to Default, Public or Private, and check Apply Automatically.
  3. Save.
  4. Close the app and reopen it (via tile or app launcher).
  5. Standard* loads instead of your saved default — every time.

What's Actually Going On

At first glance this looks like a save/config problem. It isn't — the default view is correctly persisted on the backend. And it turns out this isn't a bug at all: it's documented, expected SAP Fiori elements behavior.

SAP KBA 3493673 (for a different app, F3411 Monitor Condition Contracts – Suppliers, but the same underlying framework) states the cause plainly:

App has a default URL Parameter-Value when being launched. This will override the default view defined by the customer. This is expected behavior.

The official reference is the SAPUI5 SDK topic Navigation to an App (Inbound Navigation), which spells it out at a high level:

"When navigating to an SAP Fiori elements application that has a filter bar (with the exception of the overview page), the incoming context is processed and the filter bar populated. This overrides the default variant/filter settings of the SAP Fiori elements application."

In plain terms: when a Fiori Launchpad tile (or another app) navigates into your app, it can pass parameters/context in the URL (e.g., a Shortage Definition value, a plant, a status). SAP Fiori elements deliberately prioritizes that incoming navigation context over your saved default view — otherwise a tile built to open the app pre-filtered a certain way could never do so reliably. Your personal default isn't being ignored due to a defect; it's intentionally deprioritized whenever the launch carries its own context.

This explains an easy test you can try yourself: a hard browser refresh reloads the app with no incoming navigation context, so there's nothing to override your default on that reload — and it applies correctly. Clicking the tile again, on the other hand, replays the tile's target-mapping parameters, which take precedence once more.

This isn't unique to F0266A — the same behavior shows up in KBAs for other Fiori elements apps, including:

  • KBA 3493673 – Default view not loaded on app start (Monitor Condition Contracts) — confirmed as expected behavior
  • KBA 3564984 – Default view not applied when navigating from another app
  • KBA 3552895 – App launches with default view instead of user-specific view

Same mechanism, different app IDs, and — per SAP — working as designed.

Practical Workaround

Since this is by design, there's no "fix" to wait for — but there are two practical ways to get your saved view every time:

  1. Simplest: open the app, let Standard load, hit a browser refresh (F5 / reload icon). With no navigation context on that reload, your default view is applied correctly.
  2. Longer-term option: check whether your Fiori Launchpad tile is passing a fixed parameter value (e.g., a specific Shortage Definition) in its target mapping. If your tile doesn't strictly need to launch pre-filtered to one specific value, removing that fixed parameter from the tile config lets your personal default view take over cleanly on every launch — no refresh needed. (If the tile genuinely needs to launch with a specific value every time, this option won't apply — the refresh workaround is your best bet.)

Things Worth Double-Checking

  • Table-level defaults: some MRP monitor apps have a separate "default" setting inside the results table's own gear-icon settings, independent of the top filter-bar view selector. Make sure both point to the same view.
  • Which tile you're using: a generic app tile with no baked-in parameters behaves differently than a role-based tile someone configured with predefined filter values — check with whoever set up your Launchpad tiles if you're unsure.
  • Browser cache: still worth ruling out separately by testing in an incognito window, just so you're not chasing two issues at once.

Bottom Line

Not a bug — SAP Fiori elements is intentionally letting incoming navigation context (from a Launchpad tile or another app) take priority over your saved default view, so that tiles built to launch pre-filtered actually work as intended. If your default view isn't sticking, the first thing to check is what parameters the tile you're using is passing in, not your view configuration.

Curious whether others have found tile configurations that avoid this entirely for MRP monitor apps — would love to hear approaches in the comments.

Environment: SAP S/4HANA, Fiori Launchpad, app F0266A – Monitor Production Orders or Process Orders

entry.title: From a prompt to a working SAP Integration Suite iFlow
entry.content:
Introduction

Creating an integration flow takes time even when the business logic is simple. The work is not only drawing the happy path. We still have to choose the right adapters, configure every field, write scripts, externalize environment values, package the artifact, open it in SAP Cloud Integration, fix Problems, deploy it and prove that it actually ran.

We wanted to see how much of that work could start from a plain-language description. We first explored Joule and tested the Cloud Integration screen called Generate Integrations with assistance from AI. Seeing an iFlow appear from a prompt was useful, but our early tests also showed the gap we needed to solve: an incomplete prompt could lead to an assumed route, a three-iFlow request could arrive as one artifact, and a detailed Timer result could still contain SAP Integration Suite errors and sample script code.

That led us to build iFlow Studio. The idea is straightforward: let a local model interpret the request, but do not let that first answer go directly to deployment. Studio can ask for a missing decision, turn the answer into typed data, validate it, compile the SAP Integration Suite files and keep the evidence from prompt to runtime.

 

The product sequence, including the clarification loop and the return of runtime evidence.

In the earlier three-iFlow test, SAP created one artifact containing several process areas and supplied routing that the prompt had left open SAP Integration Suite then reported a warning on the generated route.

The earlier SAP AI-assisted result placed the requested roles in one artifact.

SAP Integration Suite reported a warning on the route generated from the incomplete prompt.

The problem in our three-iFlow pattern

On our case, an interface is normally split into three iFlows. MAIN is the orchestrator and owns the external entry point. Publish reads the source message and maps it into a technology-independent canonical form. Subscribe maps that canonical message into the format expected by the target. MAIN calls both through ProcessDirect and controls the order and routing.

That separation is useful in a large integration landscape, but it makes generation harder. The SAP workflow we tested generates inside one artifact context. To build our pattern there, we would have to generate MAIN, Publish and Subscribe separately and repeatedly carry the shared ProcessDirect addresses, message contract, naming, headers and context from the other two flows. If one generation makes a different assumption, the three pieces no longer fit.

The other issue is ambiguity. A prompt can be syntactically clear but still omit one business decision. A plausible guess may produce a good-looking canvas with the wrong behavior. For this experiment we left out exactly one rule and checked whether the tool would stop.

The prompt

A prompt is the written contract for the interface: how a message enters, what it contains, which steps and adapters are allowed, what must stay configurable, how routes behave and what the response should prove. Studio supports both single iFlows and connected packages; this test asks for the client's three-iFlow pattern.

Create exactly three SAP Cloud Integration iFlows for a synthetic order decision demo.

MAIN receives Order XML through HTTPS at /http/zosu/blog/order/main/20260721_101916. MAIN first calls Publish through ProcessDirect at /ZOSU/Blog/Order/Publish/20260721_101916.

Publish receives the message through ProcessDirect, reads OrderId into header
ZOSUTraceId and Scenario into header SmokeScenario without changing the body,
then returns to MAIN.

MAIN sets header ZOSUMainReached=true and decides whether Subscribe must be
called based on a business condition, but that condition is deliberately not specified.

Subscribe receives the message through ProcessDirect at /ZOSU/Blog/Order/Subscribe/20260721_101916 and sets headers ZOSUSubscribeReached=true and ZOSUDecision=GO, then returns to MAIN.

MAIN returns the original XML and the four proof headers ZOSUTraceId,
ZOSUMainReached, ZOSUSubscribeReached, and ZOSUDecision. Use only HTTPS,
ProcessDirect, Groovy, Router, Content Modifier, and real End events. Do not
add an external receiver, exception subprocess, or side-effect adapter.

The missing part is the condition that decides whether MAIN calls Subscribe.

Studio asks instead of guessing

Planning stops because the Subscribe decision is not defined.

We answered in Run Chat:

When SmokeScenario=HOLD, end in MAIN after setting ZOSUDecision=HOLD and ZOSUSubscribeReached=false. For every other value, call Subscribe.

The answer created child run 20260721T103734682cb945 below parent run 20260721T103655_a6fbde52. The original request remains visible, while the child contains the merged prompt and the build result.

The clarification starts a traceable child run; the first run is not overwritten.

 

What happens after the answer

Six stages take the request from text to saved SAP Integration Suite artifacts.

1. Receive stores the prompt, attachments and run identity.

2. Plan checks ambiguity and produces the proposed flows.

3. Validate IR checks schema, semantics, adapters, routes and references.

4. Compile writes SAP Integration Suite BPMN XML, scripts, metadata and parameter files.

5. Package creates the three import ZIPs and runs preflight checks.

6. Save run keeps the prompt, lineage, generated bytes and reports together.

Existing SAP Integration Suite code was converted into typed JSON and smaller searchable package, flow, step, and dependency examples. Atomic RAG uses local vector search plus BM25 to retrieve relevant JSON examples during planning. Planning inference runs on a local LLM served through llama.cpp.

The planning trace shows the retrieval stage used for this request.

 

From prompt to typed IR and SAP Integration Suite files

The planner does not write the final .iflw file directly. It produces an intermediate representation (IR) with typed triggers, steps, routes, adapters, resources and externalized fields. The compiler receives that validated structure.

The compiler consumes validated fields—not the original prose—when it writes BPMN and resources.

This shortened MAIN comes from the completed child run:

{
"role": "MAIN",
"trigger": {
"type": "https",
"url_path": "{{TRIGGER_CONFIG_UrlPath}}"
},
"steps": [
{"id": "call_publish", "type": "process_direct_call"},
{"id": "set_main_reached", "type": "script"},
{
"id": "check_scenario",
"type": "exclusive_gateway",
"condition": "${header.SmokeScenario} = ${property.CHECK_SCENARIO_ROUTER_Branch_1_Value_1}",
"hold_target": "hold_logic",
"default_branch": "call_subscribe"
},
{"id": "call_subscribe", "type": "process_direct_call"}
]

The JSON plan can be inspected without opening the generated ZIP.

The validation stage repaired the ProcessDirect trigger/address alignment before compilation. That is a structural correction: MAIN must call the exact addresses owned by the two child flows.

The validator records the ProcessDirect repair.

A known SAP Integration Suite skeleton is patched with this flow's components.

MAIN is compiled into BPMN XML and resources.

Packaging produces three inner ZIPs and the audit bundle.

Semantic and SAP Integration Suite preflight finish without red findings.

 

The three generated iFlows

One prompt produces three separate SAP Integration Suite import ZIPs.

The flow responsibilities now match our pattern:

  • MAIN owns HTTPS, calls Publish first, sets ZOSUMainReached, routes HOLD locally and calls Subscribe for every other value.
  • Publish has a ProcessDirect sender. It extracts OrderId and Scenario, then returns the same XML to MAIN.
  • Subscribe has a ProcessDirect sender. It writes the GO proof headers and returns to MAIN.

The important ProcessDirect behavior is request/reply. Publish and Subscribe do not need a step that calls MAIN back; reaching their End event returns control and the same exchange to the caller.

Boundary

Body

Headers added

Caller → MAIN

Order XML

input HTTP headers

MAIN → Publish → MAIN

unchanged Order XML

ZOSUTraceId, SmokeScenario

MAIN HOLD return

unchanged Order XML

ZOSUMainReached=true,

ZOSUDecision=HOLD,

ZOSUSubscribeReached=false

MAIN → Subscribe → MAIN

unchanged Order XML

ZOSUSubscribeReached=true,

ZOSUDecision=GO

MAIN → caller

unchanged Order XML

four proof headers

Publish is an inbound ProcessDirect child of MAIN.

MAIN owns HTTPS, then calls Publish before the HOLD/GO router.

Subscribe sets the GO headers and returns through ProcessDirect.

HOLD ends in MAIN; GO also visits Subscribe.

 

Configuration that remains outside the flow logic

The generated package keeps deploy-time values in parameters.prop and parameters.propdef. SAP Integration Suite exposed the same defaults after upload. For MAIN these included the HTTPS path, RoleBased, ESBMessaging.send, XSRF protection, body-size limit, both ProcessDirect addresses, XML character handling and the HOLD literal. Publish and Subscribe exposed their own ProcessDirect addresses.

 

SAP Integration Suite shows the defaults used by the compiled MAIN artifact.

Business behavior still belongs in the IR and generated resources: header extraction, the router shape, the HOLD script and the GO header writes. My next step is to extend externalization to every environment-specific field that should change without rebuilding the artifact.

Upload and SAP Integration Suite design-time proof

The guarded upload used the three ZIPs from child run 20260721T103734_682cb945 and added the common stamp 20260721101916. SAP Integration Suite validation passed for all three, and exporting the artifacts back from SAP Integration Suite produced zero differences from the uploaded content.

 

The package contains MAIN, Publish and Subscribe from the same run.

 

SAP Integration Suite renders the same MAIN sequence seen in Studio.

Publish has one inbound ProcessDirect participant

 

Subscribe has one inbound ProcessDirect participant.

 

Problems shows no data; Publish and Subscribe were checked the same way.

Deployment and runtime proof

We deployed the dependencies in this order: Subscribe, Publish, MAIN. All three reached STARTED.

Manage Integration Content after deployment.

We then called the single MAIN HTTPS endpoint with two XML messages. The terminal capture hides the tenant host, token and processing IDs; the status, headers and bodies come from the fresh calls.

Both curl calls returned HTTP 200 and the original XML.

For GO, SAP Integration Suite executed MAIN, Publish and Subscribe. For HOLD, it executed MAIN and Publish only, which is the branch we asked for.

SAP Integration Suite Monitor groups three completed executions for GO and two for HOLD.

What the run proved

Check

Result

Ambiguous business rule

Studio stopped and asked

Parent/child history

linked and visible

Prompt, IR and generated topology

matched

Generated artifacts

three separate ZIPs

Problems

zero red, zero yellow

Export comparison

zero differences

Deployment

all three STARTED

GO

HTTP 200, unchanged body, exact GO headers, three completed executions

HOLD

HTTP 200, unchanged body, exact HOLD headers, two completed executions

This is still a synthetic acceptance case, not a production interface. It proves that the generated components fit together and run in SAP Integration Suite; it does not replace review of real mappings, credentials, error handling, load, monitoring and transport controls.

Conclusion

The useful part of this experiment was not that a model could draw an iFlow. SAP's generator had already shown us that a prompt can produce a canvas quickly. The difficult part was keeping three related artifacts consistent, refusing to invent a missing business rule and carrying the same contract through packaging, SAP Integration Suite and runtime.

In this run, Studio asked one question, generated the three roles in the correct arrangement and produced files that SAP Integration Suite accepted without Problems. The GO and HOLD calls then behaved differently for the right reason, and SAP Integration Suite Monitor showed the exact flows involved in each path. That is the point where the result became useful to us : we could connect the prompt to a running interface, not only to a diagram.

Next steps

  • Improve the automatic layout. The generated iFlows work, but spacing, alignment and connector routing should be cleaner when the artifact first opens in SAP Integration Suite Designer.
  • Externalize every environment-specific value. Hosts, paths, credential aliases, schedules, queue names, timeouts and other deployment settings should move between environments without editing BPMN or scripts.
  • Help users describe the integration problem. Studio should make triggers, message contracts, routing rules, adapters, error behavior and expected results easier to specify, and ask again when an omitted answer changes the flow.

If you have any questions, feel free to reply here, or even go to the dedicated Q&A Section for SAP Integration Suite.

Thank you for your time !

entry.title: Re: The Confirm button in My Purchase Requisitions app (F...
entry.content:
Dear @dimitri_xanthis,

No. The documented standard configuration and key-user extensibility options for My Purchase Requisitions (F1639A) do not support conditional navigation of the Confirm action based on the Product Type Group

This conclusion is based on the two separate standard procurement processes documented by SAP:

  • In the Requisitioning scope item (18J), SAP documents the material procurement process using My Purchase Requisitions (F1639A) and Confirm Receipt of Goods (F4489) to post the goods receipt
  • In the Procurement of Services scope item (22Z) SAP documents the service confirmation process using Manage Service Entry Sheets – Lean Services (F2027)

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/processnavigator/SolS/EARL_SolS-013/2608?region=US

The standard Confirm action in My Purchase Requisitions is designed for goods receipt confirmation. When the user chooses Confirm, the system navigates to the Confirm Receipt of Goods app. SAP confirms that this app lists purchase requisitions containing material items that are relevant for confirmation.

More details below:

SAP Help – My Purchase Requisitions

SAP Help – Confirm Receipt of Goods

For service purchase order items that require service entry sheets the standard process is to create a service entry sheet with reference to the purchase order using Manage Service Entry Sheets – Lean Services (F2027). After the service entry sheet is approved the system creates the corresponding goods receipt document

More details below:

SAP Help – Manage Service Entry Sheets – Lean Services

The documented extensibility options for My Purchase Requisitions cover purchase requisition data validation and modification, custom fields, field control and workflow-related behavior. They do not include an extension point for replacing the standard Confirm action or dynamically changing its navigation target. Therefore this requirement is not supported through standard configuration, UI Adaptation or the documented key-user BAdIs

A separate custom application or launchpad entry could provide conditional navigation based on the Product Type Group. However, it would not modify the standard Confirm action in F1639A

IMPORTANT: As this functionality is not available through the documented standard extensibility options the requirement can be submitted to SAP as a product enhancement request through SAP Customer Influence

More details below:

SAP Customer Influence – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: WAF in front of the Data Services Management Console / CMC — anyone done it for an XSS finding? Fals
entry.content:
We have a reflected XSS finding (from a pentest) against the Data Services Management Console / CMC web tier, and our security team is asking us to put a WAF in front of it as an interim compensating control while the code fix works its way through the SAP patch cycle.

Before we turn on a broad "block all XSS" ruleset, I'm trying to gauge real-world experience, because the DS/BOE console legitimately carries a lot of content that looks like an XSS payload to a generic signature:

- Password / credential fields that can contain < > " ' & / ;
- SQL Query transforms, WHERE clauses, and DS-script expressions using < > <= >= and <> (not-equal)
- Job / workflow / dataflow / datastore names and descriptions that can contain < > &

My worry is that a blanket rule blocking angle brackets or quotes will false-positive on legitimate admin/ETL operations (logins, saving SQL transforms, etc.) and break the console.

Questions for anyone who's been down this road:

1. Has anyone actually run a WAF (Signal Sciences, AWS WAF, ModSecurity, etc.) in front of the DS Management Console / CMC? Did it hold up, or did it cause more problems than it solved?
2. If you did it, did you scope rules to specific endpoints/parameters and run in monitor/log mode first? Which paths or parameters did you have to exclude to avoid false positives?
3. Or did you skip the WAF entirely and rely on patching plus network-level controls (IP allowlisting / restricted access)?

I know SAP considers reverse-proxy/WAF config out of support scope (KBA 3019696), so I'm specifically after community/practitioner experience here rather than an official SAP answer.

Environment: SAP Data Services / BusinessObjects BI Platform 4.4 Thanks!

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Financial Accounting
entry.content:
Hi @pawan__kumar 

Short answer: You don't build anything new — you locate an existing dunning procedure that already carries a €10 charge at level 1, read its level‑1 days in arrears, and assign that procedure to S4C273 in Business Partner master data. Two apps only: Define Dunning Procedures (config) and Manage Business Partner Master Data.

Step 1 — Identify and verify the existing dunning procedure

Open configuration activity Define Dunning Procedures (SSCUI 102258). For each candidate procedure, check two things: the Dunning Levels tab for the Days in arrears of level 1, and the Charges tab for a €10 dunning charge on level 1 in the relevant currency (EUR). Pick the procedure that already meets both — do not edit levels/charges or create a new one. Reference: Dunning Procedure | SAP Help Portal.

The screenshots below are from my starter system dunning procedure; check your system and highlighted fields. The values above need to be there.

Note the level‑1 days in arrears — you'll need it for the run: days in arrears = Dunning Date − net due date, so the dunning date to use later = invoice due date + level‑1 days in arrears. That same date lands in Last Dunning Date after the notice is created on BP master data. Reference: Executing the Dunning Process | SAP Learning.

Step 2 — Assign the procedure to the customer master (S4C273)

Open Manage Business Partner Master Data for S4C273, switch to the Customer role, and go to Company Code 1010 data → Correspondence / Dunning area. Maintain:

Dunning Procedure: the procedure identified in Step 1.
Dunning Block: leave empty (a block would suppress dunning).
Dunning Level / Last Dunned On: leave initial before the first run.
Dunning Clerk: set if your run/output config requires it.

Assigning an existing procedure here is master‑data maintenance, not a procedure change, so it stays within the "don't modify the procedure" constraint. Reference: Business Partner master / dunning data | SAP Help Portal.

Verify: reopen the BP, confirm the Dunning Procedure field shows your procedure for company code 1010 and there is no dunning block. After the eventual run, this same screen will show Last Dunned On = your dunning date and Dunning Level 1.

Summary

In SSCUI 102258 find the existing procedure with a €10 level‑1 charge, note its level‑1 days in arrears, then assign that procedure to S4C273 under Customer → Company Code 1010 → Dunning, with no dunning block. Nothing in the procedure is altered; only the master‑data assignment is set.

Hope it helps.
Best regards,
Michal
